spot-misleading-headlines.title: Course: Spotting Misleading Headlines
spot-misleading-headlines.description: A practical course on decoding clickbait, missing context, and emotional framing before you click or share.

lessons.title: 1. Course orientation
lessons.body:
## Welcome to ML-101

In this course you will practice reading headlines the way a careful editor does: as claims to test, not facts to absorb.

## Learning goals

- Spot emotional and absolute language
- Check whether the article body supports the headline
- Build a 60-second verification habit

## How the course works

Each unit includes a short reading, examples, and a key takeaway. At the end, a short quiz checks that you can apply the skills—not just memorize definitions.

## Time estimate

About 45 minutes including the quiz. You can pause anytime; progress is saved when you mark a unit complete.

lessons.title: 2. Why headlines mislead
lessons.body:
## Attention economics

Headlines compete for clicks. That pressure rewards emotional, incomplete, or exaggerated framing—even when the article body is more careful.

## Common patterns

- ALL CAPS, exclamation points, or words like "shocking", "unbelievable", "they don't want you to know"
- Absolute claims with no source: "Scientists prove…", "Everyone is saying…"
- Missing who / when / where — the classic context gap
- Questions that imply scandal without stating a verifiable fact

## Mini exercise

Open a news app and list three headlines. For each, write one concrete fact the headline actually asserts. If you cannot, mark it "weak claim."

## Key takeaway

Treat the headline as a claim to check — not as proof.

lessons.title: 3. Headline vs. body mismatch
lessons.body:
## The bait-and-switch

Some pieces use a dramatic headline, then bury nuance in paragraph eight. Readers who only skim the headline still share the exaggerated version.

## What to check in the body

- Does it name a specific study, date, or official source?
- Are quotes complete or cut mid-sentence?
- Is language in the body more hedged ("may", "suggests", "small sample") than the headline?

## Worked example (fictional)

Headline: "Miracle herb CURES disease overnight!!!"
Body: "A 12-person pilot study found modest symptom improvement over four weeks; more research needed."

Mismatch: headline overclaims; body is cautious.

## Key takeaway

If the body is more careful than the headline, share the careful version—or don't share yet.

lessons.title: 4. The 60-second checklist
lessons.body:
## Run this every time

1. Who published this? Corrections policy and bylines?
2. Does the headline name a person, study, place, or date?
3. Can you remove emotional words and still have a concrete claim?
4. Do two independent reputable outlets report the same core fact?
5. Is this labeled opinion/analysis but styled as hard news?

## Scoring yourself

- 4–5 yes → lower risk, still verify if stakes are high
- 2–3 yes → needs verification
- 0–1 yes → treat as potentially misleading until proven otherwise

## Key takeaway

Speed is optional; a one-minute check is not.

lessons.title: 5. Practice & next steps
lessons.body:
## Capstone practice

Find one viral headline today. Apply the checklist. Write two sentences: (1) what the headline claims, (2) what evidence you found.

## Tools that help

- TrustLensAI for a second pass of signals
- Reverse search for related images
- Official sites and wire services for confirmation

## Before the quiz

You should be able to name three red-flag patterns and describe the 60-second checklist from memory.

## Remember

TrustLens scores are educational signals, not final verdicts.

questions.question: Which is a red flag in a headline?
options: States clear facts with named sources
Uses ALL CAPS and emotional words
questions.explanation: Emotional language and all-caps often signal clickbait rather than careful reporting.

questions.question: A good first step when a headline makes you angry is to:
options: Share it immediately so others know
Read the full article and check sources
Trust the first comment you see
questions.explanation: Slow down, read past the headline, and look for independent confirmation.

questions.question: If removing emotional words leaves almost no concrete claim, the headline is likely:
questions.explanation: Strong feelings without specifics are a classic misleading pattern.

questions.question: Headline–body mismatch means:
options: The headline overstates what the article actually supports
questions.explanation: When the body is more careful than the headline, the headline is often the misleading part.

questions.question: How many independent reputable outlets should ideally confirm a surprising claim before you share?
questions.explanation: Triangulating across independent sources reduces the risk of being misled.

verify-images-videos.title: Course: Verifying Images & Video
verify-images-videos.description: Trace photos and clips to their original context using reverse search, landmarks, and careful provenance checks.

lessons.title: 1. Course orientation
lessons.body:
## Welcome to VF-201

Visual media is powerful because it feels like proof. This course teaches you to test that feeling.

## Learning goals

- Detect out-of-context reuse of real photos
- Use reverse image search effectively
- Spot common deepfake/edit signals without overconfidenceing them

## Tools you may use

Browser reverse search, maps for landmarks, and reputable news archives. No paid tools required.

lessons.title: 2. Images can lie without editing
lessons.body:
## Real photo, wrong story

A genuine photograph can still mislead if paired with the wrong caption, date, or place. This is more common than elaborate forgeries.

## Red flags

- Dramatic claim with a stock-looking image
- No photographer credit or original post
- "Forwarded many times" messaging screenshots
- Video clips with no full context or original upload date

## Key takeaway

Always ask: is this media from the event being described?

lessons.title: 3. Reverse image search workflow
lessons.body:
## Practical techniques

1. Reverse image search (Google Images, TinEye, Yandex) to find earlier appearances
2. Check whether the first known use matches the claim's date and location
3. Look for EXIF/metadata when available (often stripped on social platforms)
4. For video: search distinctive phrases, signs, or landmarks in the frame
5. Prefer primary sources (official accounts, wire services, original livestreams)

## Worked example

Flood photo labeled "today in City A" reverse-searches to a three-year-old event in City B. Image real; caption false.

## Key takeaway

Provenance beats vibes: find the earliest trustworthy appearance.

lessons.title: 4. Deepfakes and heavy edits
lessons.body:
## Imperfect visual signals

- Warped hands, teeth, jewelry, or small text
- Inconsistent shadows or reflections
- Melted backgrounds; odd morphing at edges
- Mismatched lip-sync or lighting in video

## Limits of detection

Human eyes and automated detectors both err. Treat "looks AI" as a prompt to investigate, not courtroom proof.

## When stakes are high

Seek corroboration from reputable outlets and original uploads before acting or resharing.

## Key takeaway

Combine visual checks with independent reporting.

lessons.title: 5. Capstone lab
lessons.body:
## Practice assignment

1. Save one viral image from your feed
2. Reverse search it and note the earliest credible source
3. Write whether the caption matches that source
4. List one next step if you are still unsure

## Ready for the quiz

You should be able to explain out-of-context reuse, reverse search steps, and why detectors alone are not enough.

questions.question: A real photo can still mislead when it is:
options: Shown with the wrong date or location caption
questions.explanation: Out-of-context reuse is one of the most common image deceptions.

questions.question: Reverse image search is mainly used to:
options: Find earlier appearances of the same image
questions.explanation: It helps establish provenance and whether the caption matches the original context.

questions.question: Warped hands or melted backgrounds may indicate:
options: Possible AI generation or heavy editing
That the photo is automatically true
questions.explanation: These are imperfect signals that warrant further checks, not automatic proof.

questions.question: EXIF metadata on social platforms is often:
questions.explanation: Many platforms remove metadata, so absence of EXIF is common and not decisive alone.

questions.question: When visual stakes are high, you should prioritize:
questions.explanation: Corroboration from trusted independent sources reduces error risk.

understanding-ai-content.title: Course: Understanding AI-Generated Content
understanding-ai-content.description: Learn where generative AI appears online, what signals to watch for, and how to use AI checkers responsibly.

lessons.title: 1. Course orientation
lessons.body:
## Welcome to AI-210

Generative AI makes fluent text and persuasive images cheap to produce. This course builds practical AI literacy for everyday verification.

## Learning goals

- Map where AI content shows up
- Use imperfect signals without false certainty
- Treat AI tools as assistants, not authorities

lessons.title: 2. The AI content landscape
lessons.body:
## Common surfaces

- Social posts and comment spam
- Thin "news-like" rewrites
- Synthetic profile and product photos
- Fabricated quotes and political memes

## Why volume matters

AI reduces production cost. Engagement systems still reward emotion and novelty, so low-quality content can flood attention.

## Key takeaway

Fluency is not truthfulness.

lessons.title: 3. Signals in AI text
lessons.body:
## Imperfect text signals

- Generic, balanced prose with no concrete details
- Invented citations or dead links
- Overconfident claims without primary sources
- Style that shifts abruptly mid-thread

## Counterexamples

Human writing can be generic; AI can be specific. Signals are prompts to investigate, not automatic labels.

## Key takeaway

Demand sources you can open and check yourself.

lessons.title: 4. Signals in AI images & media
lessons.body:
## Imperfect visual signals

- Distorted hands, teeth, jewelry, or small text
- Inconsistent shadows or reflections
- Backgrounds that melt at edges

## Audio/video

Watch for lip-sync issues and too-clean audio for the setting. Again: imperfect signals only.

## Key takeaway

Combine visual suspicion with independent reporting.

lessons.title: 5. Using TrustLensAI responsibly
lessons.body:
## What TrustLensAI is for

It surfaces media-literacy signals and next steps. It can be wrong, incomplete, or biased.

## Good practice

- Never treat a score as legal or scientific proof
- Prefer primary documents and multiple outlets
- Disclose uncertainty when you share analysis

## Capstone

Take one AI-suspicious post and write: (1) two signals you noticed, (2) two sources you would check, (3) whether you would share yet.

## Key takeaway

AI can assist judgment; it should not replace it.

questions.question: Fluent, confident writing automatically means the content is true.
questions.explanation: AI and humans can both produce fluent falsehoods. Fluency is not evidence.

questions.question: A better response to suspected AI misinformation is to:
questions.explanation: Seek independent sources and primary documents; detection tools have limits.

questions.question: Invented citations that do not resolve are a signal of:
questions.explanation: Fake references are a known failure mode of generative text systems.

questions.question: TrustLensAI scores should be treated as:
questions.explanation: Scores support literacy; they do not replace independent verification.

questions.question: Why does AI-generated content spread quickly?
options: It is cheap to produce at high volume and often emotional
questions.explanation: Low production cost plus engagement incentives amplify volume.

source-credibility.title: Course: Source Credibility Essentials
source-credibility.description: Evaluate publishers, authors, citations, and evidence trails the way careful researchers do.

lessons.title: 1. Course orientation
lessons.body:
## Welcome to SC-120

Not every source is equal. This course builds a simple system for ranking credibility without cynicism.

## Learning goals

- Assess outlet transparency
- Follow citations to primary evidence
- Compare framing across independent sources

lessons.title: 2. Who is speaking?
lessons.body:
## Questions that matter

- Who owns or funds the publisher?
- Is there a named author with relevant expertise?
- Does the site publish corrections and contact information?
- Are claims linked to primary documents?

## Generally stronger

Wire services, peer-reviewed journals, official statistical agencies, outlets with clear editorial standards.

## Generally weaker

Anonymous blogs, pure virality, document dumps with no chain of custody, outrage-only sites.

## Key takeaway

Authority is earned through transparency and track record—not design polish alone.

lessons.title: 3. Following the evidence trail
lessons.body:
## Checklist

1. Follow links — do they support the claim?
2. Prefer primary sources over nested screenshots
3. Note publication and update dates
4. Watch for outdated studies presented as current
5. Spot single-source stories major outlets are not covering

## "Study says" claims

Find the original paper. Note sample size, funding, and limitations. Summaries often overstate results.

## Key takeaway

A citation is useful only if you can verify what it says.

lessons.title: 4. Bias without conspiracy
lessons.body:
## Framing bias

Even serious outlets choose stories and words carefully. Bias ≠ automatic fabrication, but it means you should compare frames.

## Healthy habits

- Read across the spectrum for major claims
- Separate facts (who/what/when) from interpretation
- Be extra careful when a story perfectly confirms your beliefs

## Key takeaway

Seek disconfirming evidence on purpose.

lessons.title: 5. Capstone evaluation
lessons.body:
## Assignment

Pick one article. Score it 1–5 on: transparency, primary evidence, independence, and update hygiene. Write a three-sentence credibility brief.

## Ready for the quiz

You should explain outlet transparency, primary sources, and why cross-checking frames matters.

questions.question: A transparent news outlet typically provides:
options: Bylines, contact info, and correction practices
questions.explanation: Transparency about who wrote and how errors are fixed builds accountability.

questions.question: When a post says "a study proves", you should first:
options: Find and skim the original study or official summary
questions.explanation: Media summaries often overstate findings; check the primary source.

questions.question: Comparing coverage across independent outlets helps you detect:
questions.explanation: Cross-checking reduces reliance on a single frame or error.

questions.question: Which is generally a stronger source type?
options: Peer-reviewed journal or official statistics agency
Screenshot of a screenshot of a tweet
questions.explanation: Primary, accountable sources with methods and corrections are stronger.

questions.question: Bias in mainstream outlets means:
options: You should still check framing and seek other sources
questions.explanation: Framing bias is real; it calls for comparison, not total dismissal.

think-before-sharing.title: Course: Think Before You Share
think-before-sharing.description: Build pause habits that reduce the spread of misleading content in your networks.

lessons.title: 1. Course orientation
lessons.body:
## Welcome to BH-105

Most people are not malicious—they are busy. This course builds small habits that stop harm at the share button.

## Learning goals

- Understand why speed spreads falsehoods
- Use a five-question pause routine
- Correct mistakes without shame spirals

lessons.title: 2. Why speed spreads harm
lessons.body:
## The impulse

Outrage, fear, and novelty push "share" before "check." Platforms amplify engagement, not accuracy.

## Network trust

Your friends may trust you more than a distant outlet. Your share is a recommendation.

## Key takeaway

A quick share can outrun a slow correction.

lessons.title: 3. The five-question pause
lessons.body:
## Ask before you forward

1. What emotion is this trying to create?
2. Can I name the original source?
3. Have two independent reputable outlets confirmed it?
4. What if this is false—what's the cost?
5. Am I informing or venting?

## If you cannot answer

Save the link. Verify. Decide later.

## Key takeaway

Two minutes of pause beats two days of cleanup.

lessons.title: 4. Correcting gracefully
lessons.body:
## If you shared something wrong

- Correct in the same thread when possible
- Link a better source without shaming people who trusted you
- Model the behavior you want in your community

## Culture building

Praise careful checking. Ask "source?" kindly. Use TrustLensAI as a conversation starter, not a gavel.

## Key takeaway

Humility is a media-literacy skill.

lessons.title: 5. Personal action plan
lessons.body:
## Write your rules

Create three personal rules (examples):
- No shares from headlines alone
- No health claims without a primary source
- Always reverse-search shocking images

## Capstone

For 24 hours, apply the pause routine to every share impulse. Journal one save you made by not sharing.

## Ready for the quiz

You should recite the five questions and explain why your share is a recommendation.

questions.question: Your share is best thought of as:
questions.explanation: People often trust you more than the original poster — share carefully.

questions.question: A useful pause question is:
options: How angry am I and who benefits if I share this uncritically?
How can I get more likes?
questions.explanation: Noticing emotion and incentives reduces impulsive resharing.

questions.question: If you shared something false, a responsible step is to:
options: Delete silently and hope no one noticed
Correct in the same place and link a better source
questions.explanation: Clear corrections in the same thread reduce ongoing harm.

questions.question: Platforms primarily amplify content that:
questions.explanation: Engagement incentives can favor speed and emotion over accuracy.

questions.question: The best default when you cannot verify a surprising claim is:
options: Insult people who ask for sources
questions.explanation: Delaying a share is low-cost; spreading falsehoods is not.
